categories.name: Injection Prevention
categories.description: Patterns to prevent SQL injection, command injection, and other injection attacks

categories.name: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
categories.description: Patterns to prevent XSS attacks through proper output encoding

categories.name: Authentication & Authorization
categories.description: Secure authentication patterns and access control

categories.name: Cryptography
categories.description: Proper encryption, hashing, and key management patterns

categories.name: Input Validation
categories.description: Patterns for validating and sanitizing user input

categories.name: API Security
categories.description: Secure API design patterns and best practices

categories.name: File Handling
categories.description: Secure file upload, download, and path handling

categories.name: Configuration & Secrets
categories.description: Secure configuration management and secrets handling

patterns.title: Parameterized Queries (Python/SQLite)
patterns.description: Use parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection by separating SQL logic from data.
patterns.explanation: Parameterized queries ensure that user input is treated as data, not SQL code. The database driver handles escaping automatically.

patterns.title: Parameterized Queries (Node.js/MySQL)
patterns.description: Use prepared statements with placeholder parameters in Node.js database queries.
patterns.explanation: Using placeholder '?' and passing parameters as an array prevents the database from interpreting user input as SQL commands.

patterns.title: ORM Usage (SQLAlchemy)
patterns.description: Use an ORM like SQLAlchemy to abstract database queries and prevent injection.
patterns.insecure_code:
# INSECURE - Raw SQL with string formatting
result = db.engine.execute(
    f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = '{email}'"
)
patterns.secure_code:
# SECURE - SQLAlchemy ORM
from models import User
result = User.query.filter_by(email=email).first()
patterns.explanation: ORMs automatically parameterize queries and provide a safe abstraction layer over raw SQL.

patterns.title: PreparedStatement (Java/JDBC)
patterns.description: Use PreparedStatement instead of Statement for database queries in Java.
patterns.explanation: PreparedStatement pre-compiles the SQL and separates the query structure from user data.

patterns.title: PDO Prepared Statements (PHP)
patterns.description: Use PDO prepared statements for safe database access in PHP.
patterns.explanation: PDO prepared statements bind parameters safely, preventing SQL injection regardless of input.

patterns.title: Safe Subprocess Execution
patterns.description: Use subprocess with list arguments instead of shell=True to prevent command injection.
patterns.insecure_code:
# INSECURE - Command injection
import os
def ping_host(host):
    os.system(f'ping {host}')
patterns.secure_code:
# SECURE - Subprocess with list args
import subprocess
def ping_host(host):
    # Validate input first
    import re
    if not re.match(r'^[a-zA-Z0-9.-]+$', host):
        raise ValueError('Invalid hostname')
    result = subprocess.run(
        ['ping', '-c', '4', host],
        capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=30
    )
    return result.stdout
patterns.explanation: Using subprocess.run() with a list prevents shell interpretation. Combined with input validation, this eliminates command injection.

patterns.title: HTML Output Encoding
patterns.description: Encode user data before inserting into HTML to prevent XSS.
patterns.insecure_code:
// INSECURE - Direct innerHTML
document.getElementById('output').innerHTML = userInput;
patterns.explanation: Using textContent instead of innerHTML prevents the browser from parsing user input as HTML/JavaScript.

patterns.title: React XSS Prevention
patterns.description: Avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML and use React's built-in escaping.
patterns.explanation: React automatically escapes values in JSX. If raw HTML is needed, use DOMPurify to sanitize it first.

patterns.title: Jinja2 Auto-Escaping
patterns.description: Enable auto-escaping in Jinja2 templates to prevent XSS.
patterns.secure_code:
# SECURE - Auto-escaping with Jinja2 from flask import render_template_string from markupsafe import escape @app.route('/greet') def greet(): name = request.args.get('name') # Option 1: Manual escape return f'

Hello {escape(name)}

' # Option 2: Template (auto-escapes) # return render_template_string('

Hello {{ name }}

', name=name)
patterns.explanation: Jinja2 auto-escapes template variables. Using escape() or templates prevents user input from being interpreted as HTML.

patterns.title: Secure Password Hashing (bcrypt)
patterns.description: Use bcrypt for password hashing instead of MD5/SHA.
patterns.insecure_code:
# INSECURE - MD5 hashing
import hashlib
def store_password(password):
    hashed = hashlib.md5(password.encode()).hexdigest()
    save_to_db(hashed)
patterns.explanation: bcrypt is designed for password hashing with built-in salting and adjustable work factor, making brute-force attacks impractical.

patterns.title: JWT Token Validation
patterns.description: Properly validate JWT tokens with expiration and algorithm checks.
patterns.explanation: Always set token expiration, use strong secrets from environment variables, and validate algorithm and issuer claims.

patterns.title: CSRF Token Validation (Flask)
patterns.description: Implement CSRF protection for state-changing requests.
patterns.secure_code:
# SECURE - CSRF protection with Flask-WTF
from flask_wtf.csrf import CSRFProtect
csrf = CSRFProtect(app)

@app.route('/transfer', methods=['POST'])
def transfer_funds():
    # CSRF token is automatically validated by Flask-WTF
    amount = request.form['amount']
    to_account = request.form['to']
    process_transfer(amount, to_account)

# In template:
# <form method="post">
#   <input type="hidden" name="csrf_token" value="{{ csrf_token() }}">
#   ...
# </form>
patterns.explanation: CSRF tokens ensure that state-changing requests originate from your application, not from a malicious site.

patterns.title: AES Encryption (Python)
patterns.description: Use AES-GCM for authenticated encryption instead of ECB mode.
patterns.insecure_code:
# INSECURE - ECB mode, no authentication
from Crypto.Cipher import AES
cipher = AES.new(key, AES.MODE_ECB)
encrypted = cipher.encrypt(data)
patterns.secure_code:
# SECURE - AES-GCM authenticated encryption
from cryptography.fernet import Fernet
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.ciphers.aead import AESGCM
import os

# Option 1: Fernet (recommended for simplicity)
key = Fernet.generate_key()
f = Fernet(key)
token = f.encrypt(data)
original = f.decrypt(token)

# Option 2: AES-GCM (for lower level control)
key = AESGCM.generate_key(bit_length=256)
nonce = os.urandom(12)
aesgcm = AESGCM(key)
ct = aesgcm.encrypt(nonce, data, associated_data)
patterns.explanation: AES-GCM provides both encryption and authentication, preventing tampering. Fernet wraps this in a simple, safe API.

patterns.title: Cryptographically Secure Random
patterns.description: Use secrets module instead of random for security-sensitive values.
patterns.explanation: The secrets module uses OS-provided CSPRNG, making generated values unpredictable unlike the random module which uses Mersenne Twister.

patterns.title: Email Validation
patterns.description: Properly validate email addresses using established libraries.
patterns.explanation: Email validation should check format, domain validity, and normalize the address. Use established libraries when possible.

patterns.title: Rate Limiting (Flask)
patterns.description: Implement rate limiting to prevent brute-force and denial-of-service attacks.
patterns.explanation: Rate limiting prevents automated attacks by restricting the number of requests per time window.

patterns.title: Secure CORS Configuration
patterns.description: Configure CORS properly to restrict cross-origin access.
patterns.insecure_code:
# INSECURE - Allow all origins
from flask_cors import CORS
CORS(app, resources={r'/*': {'origins': '*'}})
patterns.explanation: Restricting CORS origins prevents unauthorized domains from making API requests on behalf of your users.

patterns.title: API Authentication Middleware (Express)
patterns.description: Add authentication middleware to protect API endpoints.
patterns.explanation: Every API endpoint that handles sensitive data should verify the caller's identity through JWT or session tokens.

patterns.title: Path Traversal Prevention
patterns.description: Prevent directory traversal attacks when handling file paths.
patterns.explanation: Always validate that resolved file paths stay within the intended directory. Use secure_filename() and verify with abspath.

patterns.title: Secure File Upload
patterns.description: Validate file type, size, and content before accepting uploads.
patterns.secure_code:
# SECURE - Validated file upload
import os
from werkzeug.utils import secure_filename
import magic  # python-magic

ALLOWED_EXTENSIONS = {'png', 'jpg', 'jpeg', 'gif', 'pdf'}
MAX_SIZE = 10 * 1024 * 1024  # 10MB

def allowed_file(filename):
    return '.' in filename and \
        filename.rsplit('.', 1)[1].lower() in ALLOWED_EXTENSIONS

@app.route('/upload', methods=['POST'])
def upload():
    f = request.files.get('file')
    if not f or not f.filename:
        abort(400, 'No file provided')
    if not allowed_file(f.filename):
        abort(400, 'File type not allowed')
    # Check actual content type
    header = f.read(1024)
    f.seek(0)
    mime = magic.from_buffer(header, mime=True)
    if mime not in ['image/png', 'image/jpeg', 'image/gif', 'application/pdf']:
        abort(400, 'Invalid file content')
    # Check size
    f.seek(0, os.SEEK_END)
    if f.tell() > MAX_SIZE:
        abort(400, 'File too large')
    f.seek(0)
    filename = secure_filename(f.filename)
    f.save(os.path.join(app.config['UPLOAD_DIR'], filename))
patterns.explanation: Validate both file extension and actual content type (magic bytes). Limit file size and use secure_filename to prevent path attacks.

patterns.title: Environment Variables for Secrets
patterns.description: Store secrets in environment variables, never in source code.
patterns.insecure_code:
# INSECURE - Hardcoded credentials
DATABASE_URL = 'postgresql://admin:password123@db.example.com/prod'
API_KEY = 'sk-12345abcdef'
SECRET_KEY = 'my-secret-key'
patterns.secure_code:
# SECURE - Environment variables
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()  # Load from .env file

DATABASE_URL = os.environ['DATABASE_URL']
API_KEY = os.environ['API_KEY']
SECRET_KEY = os.environ['SECRET_KEY']

# Validate required vars at startup
required_vars = ['DATABASE_URL', 'API_KEY', 'SECRET_KEY']
for var in required_vars:
    if not os.environ.get(var):
        raise RuntimeError(f'Missing required env var: {var}')
patterns.explanation: Environment variables keep secrets out of version control. Use .env files for local development and proper secret management in production.

patterns.title: Security Headers (Flask)
patterns.description: Add security headers to protect against common web attacks.
patterns.insecure_code:
# INSECURE - No security headers
app = Flask(__name__)
patterns.secure_code:
# SECURE - Security headers middleware
app = Flask(__name__)

@app.after_request
def add_security_headers(response):
    response.headers['X-Content-Type-Options'] = 'nosniff'
    response.headers['X-Frame-Options'] = 'DENY'
    response.headers['X-XSS-Protection'] = '1; mode=block'
    response.headers['Strict-Transport-Security'] = (
        'max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains'
    )
    response.headers['Content-Security-Policy'] = (
        "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'"
    )
    response.headers['Referrer-Policy'] = 'strict-origin-when-cross-origin'
    response.headers['Permissions-Policy'] = (
        'geolocation=(), camera=(), microphone=()'
    )
    return response
patterns.explanation: Security headers instruct browsers to enable additional protections against XSS, clickjacking, MIME sniffing, and more.

patterns.title: Content Security Policy (Express)
patterns.description: Implement CSP headers to prevent inline script execution.
patterns.explanation: CSP tells the browser which resources are allowed to load, effectively preventing most XSS attacks even if vulnerabilities exist.

patterns.title: HTTPS/TLS Configuration (Node.js)
patterns.description: Enable HTTPS with proper TLS configuration.
patterns.explanation: Always use TLS 1.2+ in production. Redirect HTTP to HTTPS and use strong cipher suites.

patterns.title: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
patterns.description: Implement proper role-based access control for API endpoints.
patterns.explanation: Always verify both authentication (who you are) and authorization (what you can do). Use decorators for clean, reusable access control.

patterns.title: Input Validation and Sanitization (Go)
patterns.description: Validate and sanitize all user inputs in Go applications.
patterns.explanation: Always validate input against expected patterns and use Go's html/template package which auto-escapes output.

patterns.title: Parameterized Queries (C#/ADO.NET)
patterns.description: Use parameterized queries in C# to prevent SQL injection.
patterns.explanation: SqlCommand parameters are type-safe and automatically escaped, preventing SQL injection attacks.

patterns.title: Password Hashing (Node.js bcrypt)
patterns.description: Use bcrypt for secure password hashing in Node.js.
patterns.explanation: bcrypt is specifically designed for password hashing with built-in salting and configurable cost factor, unlike general-purpose hash functions.

patterns.title: NoSQL Injection Prevention (MongoDB)
patterns.description: Prevent NoSQL injection in MongoDB queries.
patterns.explanation: NoSQL databases accept object queries which attackers can exploit. Always validate input types and use express-mongo-sanitize.

patterns.title: Request Schema Validation (Pydantic)
patterns.description: Use Pydantic or marshmallow schemas to validate API inputs and prevent mass assignment.
patterns.insecure_code:
# INSECURE - Accept all fields
@app.route('/api/user', methods=['PUT'])
def update_user():
    data = request.get_json()
    # Directly updates all fields including 'role', 'is_admin'
    User.query.filter_by(id=user_id).update(data)
    db.session.commit()
patterns.explanation: Schema validation ensures only expected fields are accepted, preventing mass assignment attacks where users modify protected fields like roles.

patterns.title: ZIP Bomb Detection
patterns.description: Detect and prevent decompression bomb attacks when handling ZIP files.
patterns.insecure_code:
# INSECURE - No size checking
import zipfile
def extract_zip(filepath):
    with zipfile.ZipFile(filepath) as zf:
        zf.extractall('/uploads/')
patterns.secure_code:
# SECURE - ZIP bomb protection
import zipfile
import os

MAX_UNCOMPRESSED = 500 * 1024 * 1024  # 500MB
MAX_RATIO = 10  # Max compression ratio
MAX_FILES = 1000

def safe_extract_zip(filepath, dest):
    with zipfile.ZipFile(filepath) as zf:
        # Check total uncompressed size
        total_size = sum(i.file_size for i in zf.infolist())
        compressed_size = os.path.getsize(filepath)
        
        if total_size > MAX_UNCOMPRESSED:
            raise ValueError(f'Archive too large: {total_size} bytes')
        if len(zf.infolist()) > MAX_FILES:
            raise ValueError(f'Too many files: {len(zf.infolist())}')
        if compressed_size > 0 and total_size / compressed_size > MAX_RATIO:
            raise ValueError('Suspicious compression ratio (zip bomb?)')
        
        # Check for path traversal in filenames
        for info in zf.infolist():
            target = os.path.realpath(os.path.join(dest, info.filename))
            if not target.startswith(os.path.realpath(dest)):
                raise ValueError(f'Path traversal detected: {info.filename}')
        
        zf.extractall(dest)
patterns.explanation: Always verify total uncompressed size, compression ratio, file count, and paths before extracting archives.
